topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The delivery notice was taped clumsily to my door, the handwriting spidery and unfamiliar. Inside the box, wrapped in brown paper that smelled faintly of damp earth, was a heavy, sealed container. My heart began to thump against my ribs, a frantic rhythm that drowned out the distant sounds of the city. I circled the package twice, my fingers tracing the rough edges of the cardboard. Who would send something to me? I hadn't even noticed the previous occupants had moved out yet, and now this arrived. A cold prickle ran down my spine as I lifted the box. Every instinct screamed at me to leave it untouched, to pretend it didn't exist. But curiosity, that treacherous little thing, pulled me forward. With trembling hands, I wrestled the tape free. The silence in my apartment suddenly felt thick, suffocating.

[story 2]

I watched the delivery driver retreat, his footsteps echoing down the hallway like slow, deliberate drumbeats. The package sat on my welcome mat, an incongruous object in my otherwise ordinary entryway. It was addressed to 'Apartment 3B,' a unit I had only glimpsed during the lease handover. A wave of unease washed over me, making my palms slick with sweat. I kept glancing toward the window, half-expecting to see someone lurking in the shadows across the street. My breath hitched in my throat; it felt shallow, insufficient. Slowly, I retrieved the box. The weight of it felt disproportionate to its size. As I carried it into the living room, I kept my back to the door, focusing only on the dull thud of my own footsteps as I tried to decide whether to open it or simply drop it and run.

[story 3]

The sound of the doorbell jolted me from my routine. A package. I hesitated, my hand hovering over the buzzer. When I finally answered, the sight of the delivery person’s face was blurred, indistinct. The box they offered me was dark, nondescript, and it seemed to absorb the light from the hallway. A strange tension coiled tight in my stomach, tightening until it felt like a physical constraint. I took the package, my grip surprisingly weak. As I turned away, I could feel the air in the room growing heavy, charged with an unseen energy. I moved toward the kitchen, needing distance, my movements jerky and uncoordinated. I didn't dare look at the package again until I was safely behind the counter, my pulse hammering a frantic warning against my ribs.
